lessons.title: Booleans & Comparisons
lessons.concept_intro: So far, Python does exactly what you tell it, every time. But real programs need to DECIDE things. Booleans are the foundation: True or False, yes or no.
lessons.analogy: A boolean is like a light switch — it's either ON (True) or OFF (False). Comparisons are questions that Python answers with True or False.

exercise.goal: Predict what Python will say — True or False?
rules: For each comparison below, predict whether Python will say True or False
Write your prediction as a comment, then uncomment the print to check
Get at least 7 out of 8 correct!
exercise.starter_code:
# Predict: True or False? Write your guess, then uncomment to check.

# 1. Is 10 greater than 5?
# My prediction: 
print(10 > 5)

# 2. Is 3 equal to 3.0?
# My prediction: 
print(3 == 3.0)

# 3. Is "hello" equal to "Hello"?
# My prediction: 
print("hello" == "Hello")

# 4. Is 7 not equal to 7?
# My prediction: 
print(7 != 7)

# 5. Is 1 greater than or equal to 1?
# My prediction: 
print(1 >= 1)

# 6. Is 0 equal to False?
# My prediction: 
print(0 == False)

# 7. Is "" (empty string) equal to False?
# My prediction: 
print("" == False)

# 8. Is 10 less than 10?
# My prediction: 
print(10 < 10)

vocabulary.definition: A value that is either True or False
vocabulary.analogy: A light switch — ON or OFF, nothing in between
Questions you ask Python: 'Is this bigger? Equal? Different?'

vocabulary.definition: Checks if two values are EQUAL (not the same as = which assigns!)
vocabulary.analogy: Asking 'are these the same?' vs = which means 'make this equal to'

lessons.title: If / Else
lessons.concept_intro: Now that Python can answer True/False questions, we can make it DO different things based on the answer. 'if' means 'do this when True', 'else' means 'otherwise do that'.
lessons.analogy: It's like a fork in the road — if the sign says 'bridge open', go straight. Otherwise, take the detour.

exercise.goal: Write a function that checks if someone can vote
rules: Write a function can_vote(age) that:
Returns 'You can vote!' if age is 18 or older
Returns 'Not yet! You need to wait X more years.' if under 18
X should be the actual number of years left
examples: can_vote(15) → 'Not yet! You need to wait 3 more years.'
hints: Use if age >= 18: for the check

vocabulary.definition: Runs code only when a condition is True
vocabulary.analogy: IF it's raining, take an umbrella

vocabulary.definition: Runs code when the if condition is False
An expression that Python evaluates to True or False
vocabulary.analogy: The question being asked at the fork in the road

lessons.title: Elif Chains
lessons.concept_intro: Sometimes there aren't just two choices — there are many. 'elif' (short for 'else if') lets you check multiple conditions in order.
lessons.analogy: Like a restaurant menu with prices: if you have over $50, get the steak. Elif over $30, get the chicken. Elif over $15, get the pasta. Else, get the soup.

rules: Write a function get_grade(score) that:
Returns 'A' for 90-100
Returns 'B' for 80-89
Returns 'C' for 70-79
Returns 'D' for 60-69
Returns 'F' for below 60
hints: Start with the highest: if score >= 90: return 'A'
Then check each range with elif

vocabulary.definition: Short for 'else if' — checks another condition if the first was False
vocabulary.analogy: Like 'or if not that, then check this...'

lessons.title: Logical Operators
lessons.concept_intro: Sometimes one condition isn't enough. 'and' means BOTH must be true. 'or' means AT LEAST ONE must be true. 'not' flips True to False and vice versa.
lessons.analogy: 'and' = both hands must be raised. 'or' = at least one hand raised. 'not' = put your hand DOWN if it's up.

rules: This code has 3 bugs — find and fix them all
The function should return True only if height >= 120 AND age >= 8
Read the code carefully, figure out what's wrong, and fix it
exercise.test_calls:
print(can_ride(130, 10))   # Should be True
    print(can_ride(110, 10))   # Should be False
    print(can_ride(130, 6))    # Should be False
    print(can_ride(100, 5))    # Should be False
    print(can_ride(120, 8))    # Should be True

vocabulary.definition: Both conditions must be True for the whole thing to be True
vocabulary.analogy: Like needing BOTH a ticket AND an ID to enter

vocabulary.definition: At least one condition must be True
vocabulary.analogy: Like accepting cash OR card — either works

vocabulary.definition: Flips True to False and False to True

lessons.title: Nested Conditions
lessons.concept_intro: Sometimes you need to check a condition INSIDE another condition. This is called nesting — an if inside an if.
lessons.analogy: Like airport security: first check — do you have a boarding pass? If yes, second check — any liquids over 100ml?

exercise.goal: Build a movie ticket pricer with multiple rules
rules: Write a function ticket_price(age, is_student, day) that returns the price:
Base prices: child (under 12) = 6, adult = 12, senior (65+) = 8
Students get 2 off adult price (must be 12-64 to be student)
On 'tuesday', everyone gets 2 off their price
Minimum price is always 4 (discounts can't go below that)
hints: Start by determining the base price from age
Then apply student discount if applicable
Finally, use max(price, 4) to enforce the minimum
exercise.test_calls:
print(ticket_price(10, False, 'monday'))
    print(ticket_price(25, True, 'monday'))
    print(ticket_price(25, False, 'tuesday'))
    print(ticket_price(25, True, 'tuesday'))
    print(ticket_price(70, False, 'tuesday'))
    print(ticket_price(8, False, 'tuesday'))

vocabulary.definition: Code placed inside other code — like an if inside another if
vocabulary.analogy: Russian nesting dolls — one inside the other

project.title: Text Adventure Game
project.description: Build a simple choose-your-own-adventure game! The player reads a story and makes choices that lead to different outcomes.
steps: Write the opening scene and print it
Ask the player to make a choice (e.g., 'Go left or right?')
Use if/elif/else to branch to different scenes based on choice
Add at least 2 more decision points (nested ifs)
Create at least 3 different endings (win, lose, secret)
bonus: Add an inventory system (store items in variables)
Make some choices depend on previous choices
